topic: The Impact of the Printing Press on Knowledge and Communication
title: The Movable-Type Printing Press: A Technological Innovation in 15th-Century Europe

lesson.learn: On February 23, 1455, Johannes Gutenberg, a German goldsmith and printer, completed the first printed book using movable-type technology, the Gutenberg Bible. This innovation enabled rapid reproduction of texts, with an estimated 300-400 pages per day, compared to the three to four years required for a scribe to hand-copy a single manuscript.
lesson.simpler: The movable-type printing press allowed for the efficient reproduction of texts, making it possible to mass-produce books, which in turn increased literacy rates and facilitated the spread of ideas across the globe.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The printing press played a crucial role in disseminating knowledge, contributing to increased literacy rates and the rise of a literate middle class. The Gutenberg Bible, the world's first printed book, was a 1,286-page, 42-line-per-page, 800-character-set publication, exemplifying the precision and durability of the movable-type printing press.
lesson.deeperDive: The movable-type printing press employed a combination of metal type and oil-based inks. Metal type allowed for precise font reproduction, while oil-based inks ensured durability and color intensity. The introduction of these innovations enabled the rapid reconfiguration of type pieces, facilitating the mass production of texts. The printing press contributed to the spread of scientific knowledge, such as the work of Galileo Galilei, whose Dialogue Concerning the Two Chief World Systems was widely disseminated across Europe.
lesson.funFact: The first recorded mention of the printing press in Europe was on January 24, 1455, in Mainz, Germany, where Johannes Gutenberg was granted a patent for his innovation.
